title: Radiation Survey Reports
narrationScript: A radiation survey protects you in the moment you make it. The survey report is what proves it happened — to your radiation safety officer, to an NRC or Agreement State inspector, and to anyone reconstructing dose after an incident. This lesson covers the surveys the regulations require and the report that documents them. Ten CFR 34.49 demands a calibrated, operable instrument and a survey of the exposure device and guide tube after every exposure, made while approaching, before anyone exchanges film, repositions the exposure head, or breaks down equipment. The device is surveyed again at each source exchange and whenever it goes into storage — and when that storage survey is the last of the workday, 10 CFR 34.85 requires a written record kept for three years. A defensible report identifies the job, the people, the source and device, the instrument and its calibration, every reading with its location, and an area sketch — signed by the person who made the survey. Record what the meter read, never what you expected it to read.

sections.heading: The Report Is the Proof
sections.body:
10 CFR 20.1003 defines a **survey** as an evaluation of the radiological conditions and potential hazards of using radioactive material — including, when appropriate, physical measurements of radiation levels. The meter reading protects you in the moment you take it. The **report** is what proves the survey happened, and it does three jobs no memory can do:

- **Compliance evidence.** NRC and Agreement State inspectors verify your program through records. The report is how a boundary set correctly in March is still defensible in an inspection two years later.
- **Dose reconstruction.** After a suspected overexposure or a source-out event, investigators rebuild who could have received what from the survey reports — readings, locations, and times.
- **Trend and audit material.** The RSO uses accumulated reports to catch drifting technique, creeping device-surface readings, or a jobsite that needs a different setup.

The working rule of every radiation safety program: **a survey that was never recorded earns no compliance credit.** If it is not written down, it did not happen — and the radiographer who made the survey is responsible for writing it down completely, at the time of the survey, not from the truck at the end of the week.

sections.heading: The Surveys Behind Every Report
sections.body:
Every required survey rides on the same instrument basis: a **calibrated and operable** survey meter meeting 10 CFR 34.25 — able to read from **2 mR/h through 1 R/h**, calibrated at intervals **not exceeding 6 months** and after servicing (battery changes excepted), with demonstrated accuracy within **±20%**. Readings taken with anything less prove nothing.

10 CFR 34.49 then requires the surveys your report documents:

- **After each exposure** — survey the **exposure device and the guide tube while approaching them**. The survey must determine that the sealed source has returned to its shielded position **before** anyone exchanges film, repositions the exposure head, or dismantles equipment. This is the survey that catches a stuck or disconnected source while distance is still working in your favor.
- **At every source exchange** — survey the device to confirm the source sits where the shielding is.
- **Whenever the device is placed in a storage area** — truck compartment, darkroom, or vault, the device is surveyed going in.

Beyond Part 34, 10 CFR 20.1501 requires surveys that are **necessary to comply and reasonable under the circumstances** — in field radiography, that is the boundary survey: walking the restricted-area perimeter with the source exposed and recording the dose rates that justify where you put the rope.

sections.caption: The surveys 10 CFR 34.49 requires: after every exposure (device and full guide tube, made while approaching), at every source exchange, and whenever the device is placed in storage — and the last storage survey of the workday must be recorded.

sections.heading: Which Records Are Required — and for How Long
sections.body:
The regulations set a floor for which surveys become written records and how long those records live:

- **10 CFR 34.85** — a record is required of the exposure-device survey made before storage under 34.49(c) **if it is the last survey performed in the workday**. Retention: **3 years** after the record is made.
- **10 CFR 20.2103(a)** — records of the surveys required by 20.1501 — your area and boundary surveys — are kept **3 years** after the record is made.
- **10 CFR 20.2103(b)** — a survey used, alone or together with dosimetry data, **to assess an individual's dose** must be kept **until the NRC terminates the license**. The boundary report from an ordinary Tuesday becomes a permanent record the moment it feeds an overexposure evaluation.
- **10 CFR 34.89(b)** — specific documents must physically be available **at each field station and temporary jobsite**: the latest survey records, evidence of the current calibration of the survey meters in use, utilization logs, operating and emergency procedures, and the license itself. "It's back at the office" is a violation, not an answer.

License conditions and Agreement State rules commonly go further — most radiography licensees require a completed survey report for **every jobsite, every shift**. The regulations set the floor; your license and written operating procedures define the form you actually fill out.

sections.caption: The standard radiation survey report, block by block. The regulations set the floor; the licensee's operating procedures define the exact form.
rows: Job / licensee header
Licensee name and license number, date, time, jobsite location
Ties the record to one job, one jurisdiction, one moment in time
Personnel
Radiographer and assistant names; signature of the individual who surveyed
Accountability — who measured and who certifies the record
Source and device
Radionuclide, current activity, source serial; device make, model, serial
Defines what the readings should be compared against
Survey instrument
Make, model, serial number, calibration date and due date
Readings count only from a calibrated, operable meter (10 CFR 34.25, 34.65)
Dose-rate readings
Each measured value with units and the exact location where it was taken
The compliance evidence itself — boundaries, postings, public dose
Area sketch
Source position, collimator direction, boundaries, reading points
Lets a reviewer reconstruct the setup years later
Storage / closeout
End-of-day storage survey of the device and container
The record 10 CFR 34.85 explicitly requires — kept 3 years

sections.caption: Anatomy of a completed radiation survey report: identification, source and device, instrument and calibration, readings with locations, the area sketch, and the surveyor's certification.

sections.heading: Dose-Rate Entries and the Boundaries They Prove
sections.body:
The heart of the report is the readings block — and the discipline is to record **numbers with units and locations**, never checkmarks. "OK" proves nothing; **"1.6 mR/h at the north boundary rope, 14:05"** proves compliance. A complete set of entries for a field job:

- **Device readings** — at the device surface and along the guide tube after the final retraction, confirming the source is shielded. An abnormally high device-surface reading flags a shielding or source-position problem.
- **Boundary readings** — at the restricted-area perimeter, supporting where the boundary was set. In any **unrestricted area** the dose must not exceed **2 mrem in any one hour**, and a member of the public is limited to **100 mrem in a year** (10 CFR 20.1301).
- **Area classifications** — your numbers establish what you posted: where levels could exceed **5 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm** is a **radiation area** (CAUTION, RADIATION AREA); where they could exceed **100 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm** is a **high radiation area** (CAUTION — or DANGER — HIGH RADIATION AREA). 10 CFR 34.53 requires every area where radiography is performed to be posted per 20.1902(a) and (b), and the posting exceptions of 20.1903 **do not apply** to radiography.

The **area sketch** ties it together: source position, collimator direction, the boundary line, and a mark for each reading keyed to the table — so a reviewer can stand exactly where you stood.

sections.caption: Plan view of a field setup: the recorded boundary readings are what demonstrate that area classifications, postings, and public dose limits were actually met.

sections.title: Record Only What You Measured
sections.body: Pre-filling readings before the survey, copying yesterday's numbers, or reconstructing a missed reading from memory turns a safety record into a false one. NRC rules require records to be **complete and accurate in all material respects** (10 CFR 30.9), and the deliberate-misconduct rule (10 CFR 30.10) lets the NRC take enforcement action against the **individual radiographer** — not just the licensee. If you discover a missed entry, leave it blank, annotate what happened, and tell your RSO. An honest gap is a procedure problem. An invented number is a federal violation.

figures.title: Anatomy of a radiation survey report
Boundaries the recorded readings must prove

interactive.title: Close Out the Shift: A Report That Holds Up
interactive.intro: Last exposure of the day at a tank-farm jobsite — Ir-192 in a crank-out projector, two boundary readings already logged from earlier shots, and an Agreement State inspector known to be working the area. Finish the day so your paperwork survives scrutiny.

steps.prompt: The final shot is done. You crank the source back, then survey the projector and the full guide tube while approaching — readings drop to the device's normal profile. An hour later the camera goes into the truck's storage compartment. Your assistant asks: "We already surveyed after the shot — are we done measuring?"
options: Yes — the post-exposure survey covers everything for the day
No — the device is surveyed again when it is placed in storage, and since that is the last survey of the workday, it must be recorded
Only if the alarming ratemeter went off during the shot
Surveys are only repeated when the source is exchanged
steps.feedback: 10 CFR 34.49(c) requires a survey whenever the device is placed in a storage area — the truck compartment counts. And because that survey is the last one performed in the workday, 10 CFR 34.85 requires a written record of it, retained for 3 years. The post-exposure survey protected you an hour ago; the storage survey and its record protect the public all night.

steps.prompt: You start the survey report. Which set of entries makes this a record an inspector can verify rather than just believe?
options: Final readings only — the dose rates are all that matter
Date and your signature — everything else is on file at the office
Date, time, and location; source and device identification; the survey instrument with its calibration status; each reading with where it was taken; the area sketch; and your name and signature
A single note reading "all surveys satisfactory" to keep the form clean
steps.feedback: A verifiable record answers every question on its own: who measured, with what instrument (and proof it was calibrated and operable per 10 CFR 34.25), what source and device were involved, what each reading was, and exactly where it was taken. Readings without an identified, in-calibration instrument — or without locations — cannot be reconstructed or defended.

steps.prompt: Flipping back through the form, you find you never wrote down the 14:00 boundary reading. You remember it was "about 1.6 mR/h." What do you do?
options: Enter 1.6 mR/h — your memory is only a few hours old
Enter 2.0 mR/h to be conservative
Copy the 10:00 boundary value, since conditions were the same
Leave the entry blank, annotate that the reading was taken but not recorded, and report it to the RSO
steps.feedback: A number written down later from memory is not a measurement — it is a guess dressed up as one. NRC-required information must be complete and accurate in all material respects (10 CFR 30.9), and deliberately entering data you did not measure exposes you personally to enforcement under 10 CFR 30.10. An annotated gap is a procedure problem your RSO can fix; a fabricated entry is a violation no one can.

steps.prompt: Next morning the inspector meets your crew at the new jobsite and asks for your radiation-survey documentation. What must be available right there at the temporary jobsite?
options: Nothing — records live at the home office, and the licensee has 30 days to produce them
The latest survey records, plus evidence of the current calibration of the survey meters in use
Only the license document itself; survey records are internal paperwork
Whatever documents the radiographer personally chooses to carry
steps.feedback: 10 CFR 34.89(b) lists documents that must be maintained at each field station and temporary jobsite: the latest survey records required by 34.85, evidence of the latest calibration of the instruments in use, utilization logs, operating and emergency procedures, a copy of the license, and more. Field radiography carries its compliance file with it.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.49, a radiation survey of the exposure device and guide tube must be made:
options: Once at the start of each workday, before the first exposure
Only when the alarming ratemeter indicates the source did not retract
After each exposure, while approaching the device — confirming the source is shielded before exchanging film, repositioning the exposure head, or dismantling equipment
Weekly, on a schedule set by the radiation safety officer
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.49(b) requires a survey of the device and the guide tube after each exposure, made when approaching them — it must determine that the sealed source has returned to its shielded position before film exchange, repositioning, or teardown begins.

checkQuestions.question: Which survey record does 10 CFR 34.85 specifically require licensees to keep?
options: The survey made when the device is placed in storage, if it is the last survey performed in the workday — retained for 3 years
Every post-exposure survey of the device and guide tube, retained for 5 years
Only surveys in which a reading exceeded 2 mR/h
Only surveys performed at permanent radiographic installations
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.85 requires a record of each exposure-device survey conducted before the device is placed in storage under 34.49(c) when that survey is the last one performed in the workday, and the record must be kept for 3 years after it is made.

checkQuestions.question: A boundary survey was used, together with dosimetry data, to assess an individual's dose after a suspected overexposure. Under 10 CFR 20.2103, that survey record must be kept:
options: 90 days
1 year after the investigation closes
3 years after the record is made
Until the Commission terminates the license
checkQuestions.explanation: Routine survey records are kept 3 years under 20.2103(a), but 20.2103(b) requires records of surveys used — alone or with individual monitoring data — to assess individual dose equivalents to be retained until the NRC terminates the license.

checkQuestions.question: A recorded reading shows an accessible area where an individual could receive 60 mrem in 1 hour at 30 cm from the source. On the report and at the site, that area is a:
options: Unrestricted area — no posting required
Radiation area — posted CAUTION, RADIATION AREA
High radiation area — posted DANGER, HIGH RADIATION AREA
Very high radiation area — posted GRAVE DANGER
checkQuestions.explanation: A radiation area is one where an individual could receive more than 5 mrem (0.05 mSv) in 1 hour at 30 cm; the high-radiation-area threshold is 100 mrem (1 mSv) in 1 hour at 30 cm. At 60 mrem in an hour the area is a radiation area and is posted CAUTION, RADIATION AREA per 10 CFR 20.1902(a).

checkQuestions.question: Survey report forms include the survey meter's serial number and calibration due date because:
options: the meter manufacturer's warranty depends on recording them
regulators assess violations differently by instrument model
readings demonstrate compliance only if made with a calibrated, operable instrument — calibrated at intervals not exceeding 6 months and accurate within ±20% (10 CFR 34.25)
it allows any crew member to adjust the calibration in the field
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.49(a) requires surveys to be made with a calibrated and operable instrument meeting 34.25 — calibrated at least every 6 months and after servicing, demonstrably accurate within ±20%. Identifying the exact meter and its calibration status on the report is what makes every recorded reading defensible.

references: 10 CFR Part 34, Licenses for Industrial Radiography and Radiation Safety Requirements for Industrial Radiographic Operations (§§ 34.25, 34.49, 34.53, 34.85, 34.87, 34.89)
10 CFR Part 20, Standards for Protection Against Radiation (§§ 20.1003, 20.1301, 20.1501, 20.1902, 20.2103)
ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Appendix A §4.0 (Radiation Survey Reports)
